questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: "This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue" misses the bedside risk because a client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation" fits the safer response to a client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well" moves care toward routine care before bedside safety action.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: Because the priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse suggests renal injury, "Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight" would not correct the active problem. Renal protection depends on hydration, medication review, and creatinine follow-up before assuming the study is low risk.
distractorRationales.c: "Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn" misses the bedside risk because sodium 128 mEq/L points to renal injury. Renal protection depends on hydration, medication review, and creatinine follow-up before assuming the study is low risk.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature teaching instead of renal protection. The stem cue "A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg" makes renal injury the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine" fits the safer response to a client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: Because the causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Document the event and continue monitoring" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention" fits the safer response to the nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away.
distractorRationales.c: "Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention" fits the safer response to a client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes" moves care toward routine observation before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.c: "Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response" moves care toward reassurance before circulation and perfusion support.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: Because c diff spore precautions infection suggests infection progression, "Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "c diff spore precautions infection" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "c diff spore precautions infection" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before diagnostic reassurance.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone" makes infection progression the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a client with a chest tube notes continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber. What is the nurse's priority action?

options.text: Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak.
Clamp the chest tube near the client's chest for 30 minutes.
Add more sterile water to the suction-control chamber.
Strip the tubing toward the drainage system.

questions.rationale: Continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber suggests an air leak somewhere in the system. The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. Prolonged clamping can cause a tension pneumothorax, and stripping the tubing is not recommended because it can increase intrathoracic pressure.

distractorRationales.b: "Clamp the chest tube near the client's chest for 30 minutes" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Add more sterile water to the suction-control chamber" misses the bedside risk because the nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak" fits the safer response to the nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional.

questions.takeaway: Continuous water-seal bubbling means assess the system for an air leak before doing anything that could trap air in the chest.

questions.stem: A client with a short leg cast reports increasing pain that is not relieved by opioids. The toes are pale and the client reports numbness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Notify the provider immediately about possible compartment syndrome.
Apply ice over the cast and reassess after the next pain dose.
Elevate the leg above the level of the heart.
Loosen the cast with scissors at the bedside.

questions.rationale: Pain out of proportion, pallor, and numbness suggest compartment syndrome, which can threaten tissue viability and limb function. Immediate provider notification and urgent evaluation are needed. Comfort-only measures or excessive elevation can worsen ischemia or delay definitive treatment.

distractorRationales.b: Because pain that outpaces the injury plus pallor or numbness under a cast should trigger concern for compartment syndrome suggests medication toxicity, "Apply ice over the cast and reassess after the next pain dose" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.c: "Elevate the leg above the level of the heart" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Pain that outpaces the injury plus pallor or numbness under a cast should trigger concern for compartment syndrome" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Loosen the cast with scissors at the bedside" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before medication-safety intervention.

questions.takeaway: Pain that outpaces the injury plus pallor or numbness under a cast should trigger concern for compartment syndrome.

questions.stem: A toddler with croup has a barky cough and now has inspiratory stridor at rest. Which finding most concerns the nurse?

options.text: Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing.
Hoarse cry that improves when the child rests.
A barking cough that occurs mainly at night.
Mild fever of 100.4 F (38 C).

questions.rationale: Stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness alone. The nurse should recognize this as a potential respiratory emergency because increasing work of breathing can quickly progress to fatigue and failure. Mild fever is common and not the most urgent finding here.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing" fits the safer response to mild fever is common and not the most urgent finding here.
distractorRationales.c: "A barking cough that occurs mainly at night" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: "Mild fever of 100.4 F (38 C)" misses the bedside risk because stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: In croup, stridor at rest is the red-flag sign of worsening airway obstruction.

questions.stem: An older adult who was oriented this morning is now disorganized, trying to climb out of bed, and seeing insects on the wall. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client is showing acute delirium that needs immediate assessment.
This is expected sundowning and can wait until morning.
The client is demonstrating stable chronic dementia.
This behavior is best managed by limiting all stimulation and delaying assessment.

questions.rationale: A rapid change in mental status with agitation and visual hallucinations is more consistent with delirium than chronic dementia. Delirium can be triggered by infection, hypoxia, medications, or metabolic problems and requires prompt assessment for reversible causes. Delaying evaluation increases the risk that a treatable medical problem will be missed.

distractorRationales.b: "This is expected sundowning and can wait until morning" misses the bedside risk because delirium can be triggered by infection, hypoxia, medications, or metabolic problems and requires prompt assessment for reversible causes points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: "The client is demonstrating stable chronic dementia" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "A rapid change in mental status with agitation and visual hallucinations is more consistent with delirium than chronic" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "A rapid change in mental status with agitation and visual hallucinations is more consistent with delirium than chronic" makes infection progression the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Sudden confusion and hallucinations in an older adult point to delirium until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A client taking digoxin reports nausea, poor appetite, and seeing yellow halos around lights. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold the digoxin and assess the apical pulse.
Give the dose with food to reduce gastric upset.
Teach the client that visual changes are expected.
Administer an antacid and reassess in 1 hour.

questions.rationale: Nausea and yellow visual halos are classic early signs of digoxin toxicity. The nurse should withhold the medication and assess the apical pulse before giving any additional doses. Telling the client the findings are expected or simply treating stomach upset misses a potentially dangerous cardiac medication problem.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Nausea and yellow visual halos are classic early signs of digoxin toxicity" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Nausea and yellow visual halos are classic early signs of digoxin toxicity" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: Because a client taking digoxin reports nausea, poor appetite suggests circulation or perfusion, "Administer an antacid and reassess in 1 hour" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Suspected digoxin toxicity means hold the dose first and assess before giving more.
